calendar.matters:
MASTER CALENDAR COURT
Off Calendar Nov-15-2019 Vacated

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Notice Of Motion And Motion For Summary Judgment
Off Calendar Oct-28-2019 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF WELLS FARGO BANK, N.A.'s MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Pursuant to CCP 437C, or in the alternative, summary adjudication pursuant to CCP 437C(F).

Plaintiff Wells Fargo Bank, N.A.'s motion for summary judgment is granted. Plaintiff is entitled to the principal of $8,582.45 and costs of $815 for a total judgment of $9,397.45.

Plaintiff has maintained its burden of production on each of its six breach of contract and common counts claims based upon plaintiff's deposition testimony and the declaration of plaintiff's qualified witness (Carol Rowe).

The court overrules defendant's objections to the Rowe declaration. Ms. Rowe properly lays her foundation to testify regarding the business records that meet the business records exception of Evidence Code, 1271. (Rowe Dec., 1-6; People v. Dorsey (1974) 43 Cal.App.3d 953, 960 ["[W]e believe that bank statements prepared in the regular course of banking business and in accordance with banking regulations are in a different category than the ordinary business and financial records of a private enterprise."]; Evid. Code, 1552(a) ["A printed representation of computer information or a computer program is presumed to be an accurate representation of the computer information or computer program that it purports to represent."]) Ms. Rowe establishes that: (1) the documents were made in the ordinary course of business (Rowe Dec., 4, 11, 12, and 13); (2) the documents were made at or near the time, act, condition or event (id. 4, 12, and 14); (3) the identity and mode of preparation of the documents is provided (id. 4, 5, and 12); and (4) the sources of information and method of preparation are trustworthy. (Id. 4, 5, and 12.) These documents demonstrate how much is owed on the account. Defendant's reliance on People v. Sanchez (2016) 63 Cal.4th 665 is misplaced as the instant case does not implicate expert testimony. This is a simple collection action involving the business records exception to the hearsay rule.

Defendant's deposition testimony demonstrates that: (1) she had one credit card account with plaintiff (Larsen Depo., 9:12-10:1); (2) she admits that she owes money on the account but is unsure regarding the amount (id. at 11:15-12:6); and (3) she received billing statements from plaintiff, reviewed them, never contested the balance or late fees, and the only reason she stopped making payments was because she could not afford them. (Id. at 14:1-15:18.)

In opposition, defendant declares that she does not owe any money or she owes less and she did not agree to the adduced interest rates or late fees. This declaration contradicts the above testimony and is excluded. "A party cannot create an issue of fact by a declaration which contradicts his prior pleadings. [Citation.] In determining whether any triable issue of material fact exists, the trial court may, in its discretion, give great weight to admissions made in deposition and disregard contradictory and self-serving affidavits of the party." (Preach v. Monter Rainbow (1993) 12 Cal.App.4th 1441, 1451.)

Order and Judgment are signed. (see scanned order and judgment for details)

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Not Reported. =(302/EPS)
